title: Frankenstein; or, The Modern Prometheus
author: Mary Shelley
overture: A ship's captain, frozen in the Arctic ice, takes aboard a dying man with a story to tell. From a house in Geneva to a laboratory in Ingolstadt, across the Alps and down the Rhine, up through England and Scotland to a bare rock in the Orkneys, and at last onto the frozen sea itself — Frankenstein follows a maker and the thing he made, each chasing the other to the top of the world. Two threads run the length of the book; the map plays them together.

chapters.title: Letters I–IV — Walton to his sister
Chapter 1 — Victor's family; Elizabeth
Chapter 2 — Boyhood at Belrive; the lightning-struck oak
Chapter 3 — His mother's death; leaving for Ingolstadt
Chapter 4 — The university; the secret of life
Chapter 5 — A dreary night of November: the creature wakes
Chapter 6 — Clerval nurses him; Elizabeth's letter
Chapter 7 — Word of William's murder; home to Geneva
Chapter 8 — Justine's trial and execution
Chapter 9 — Grief; retreat to Belrive
Chapter 10 — Chamounix; the creature on the Mer de Glace
Chapter 11 — The creature's tale: awakening in the woods
Chapter 12 — The De Lacey cottage; learning to be human
Chapter 13 — Safie; learning language and history
Chapter 14 — The De Laceys' story
Chapter 15 — Reading; the attempt at friendship
Chapter 16 — Rejection; the fire; the road to Geneva; William
Chapter 17 — The demand: make me a companion
Chapter 18 — Consent; the journey to England begins
Chapter 19 — Down the Rhine; London; north to Scotland
Chapter 20 — The Orkney island; the female destroyed; adrift to Ireland
Chapter 21 — Ireland: accused of Clerval's murder
Chapter 22 — Acquitted; home; the wedding planned
Chapter 23 — The wedding night at Evian; Elizabeth killed
Chapter 24 — The vow of vengeance; the pursuit north
Walton, in continuation — the ice; Victor's death; the creature's farewell

characters.name: Victor Frankenstein
characters.role: The maker. From Geneva to Ingolstadt and back, then across Europe and the Arctic, chasing the thing he made

characters.name: The creature
characters.role: The being made at Ingolstadt. It has its own journey — to a cottage in Germany, to Geneva, and at last onto the ice, always a step ahead of its maker

characters.name: Robert Walton
characters.role: The Arctic explorer whose letters frame the book. He finds Victor on the ice and writes the whole story down

characters.name: Henry Clerval
characters.role: Victor's dearest friend, who nurses him at Ingolstadt and travels with him to England and Scotland — and is murdered for it

characters.name: Elizabeth Lavenza
characters.role: Victor's adopted cousin and bride, who waits at Geneva through every absence — and is killed on their wedding night

locations.name: Geneva
locations.story: Victor's home city, and the still centre the whole book keeps returning to. He is raised here with Elizabeth; here William is murdered and Justine hanged for it; here the family gathers, grieves, and at last plans the wedding.
locations.quote: We possessed a house in Geneva, and a _campagne_ on Belrive

image.caption: Geneva and the Pont du Mont-Blanc, with the Salève beyond — steel engraving, c.1850
image.credit: Ludwig Rohbock (del.) & Fr. Müller (sc.), pub. G. G. Lange, Darmstadt, c.1850 · Swiss National Library / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Belrive, Lake Geneva
locations.note: The Frankensteins' lakeside 'campagne' east of the city. Shelley names Belrive on the eastern shore of the lake near Cologny; this point sits on that shore, a best fit for the family's country house.
locations.story: The family's country house on the eastern shore. It is here, as a boy, that Victor watches lightning shatter an old oak and first turns his mind to the forces that will undo him; here too the household retreats after Justine's death.
locations.quote: About this time we retired to our house at Belrive

image.caption: A villa on the shore of Lake Geneva, the Alps beyond — steel engraving 'Lake of Geneva', 1840
image.credit: William Miller after D. McKenzie, 1840 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Plainpalais, Geneva
locations.story: The open ground just outside the old city walls where little William is found strangled — and where, in a flash of lightning that night, Victor first glimpses the creature he made and knows at once who the murderer is.
locations.quote: the murderer of my brother
locations.imageBlank: The only contemporaneous view of the Plaine de Plainpalais is a festive fête, tonally wrong for the open ground where little William is found strangled; a blank is more honest.

locations.name: Chamonix
locations.story: The Alpine valley Victor flees to in his grief. From here he climbs to Montanvert, hoping the sublime scenery will quiet his mind — and instead finds the creature waiting for him on the ice.
locations.quote: entered the valley of Chamounix

image.caption: The Mer de Glace above the Chamonix valley — photochrom, c.1900
image.credit: Detroit Publishing Co. photochrom, c.1900 · Library of Congress / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Montenvers & the Mer de Glace
locations.story: The glacier viewpoint above Chamonix, over the great river of ice. Here the creature bounds across the crevasses to meet its maker face to face, and — for the length of nine chapters — tells its own story: the cottage, the rejection, the murder of William.
locations.quote: vast river of ice, wound among its dependent mountains

image.caption: The Mer de Glace from Montanvert, with the hut on the moraine — hand-coloured view, c.1781
image.credit: Carl Ludwig Hackert, c.1781 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Évian-les-Bains
locations.story: The little town on the far shore of Lake Geneva where Victor and Elizabeth stop on their wedding night. Sure the creature's threatened vengeance is meant for him, Victor keeps watch — and hears Elizabeth scream in the room he has left her in.
locations.quote: sleeping that night at Evian and continuing our voyage on the following day

image.caption: Évian-les-Bains from the lake, the mountains behind — photochrom, c.1900
image.credit: Photoglob Zürich / Photochrom Print Collection, c.1900 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Ingolstadt
locations.story: The Bavarian university where Victor studies natural philosophy and, on a dreary night of November, gives his creature life — then flees the room in horror. When he returns, it is gone. Years later he comes back to buy the instruments for a second, female creature.
locations.quote: It was on a dreary night of November that I beheld the accomplishment of my toils

image.caption: Ingolstadt from the fields, the Liebfrauenmünster rising over the town — hand-coloured lithograph, 1823
image.credit: Adolph Kunike, 1823 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: The De Lacey cottage (Germany)
locations.novelName: the cottage of the De Laceys
locations.note: The novel never fixes the cottage — the creature wanders from Ingolstadt through woods and fields for some days before finding it, so it lies somewhere in the German countryside beyond the city. This point is an editorial best guess in the country north-east of Ingolstadt; the position is not the novel's.
locations.story: The hovel where the creature shelters, watching a poor blind man and his children through a chink in the wall. Here it learns to speak, to read, to hope for kindness — and, when it finally shows itself, is beaten and driven out. Its rejection here turns it toward revenge.
locations.quote: The name of the old man was De Lacey.

image.caption: The Bavarian countryside on the Danube, near Ingolstadt — oil painting, 1863
image.credit: Pieter Franciscus Peters, 1863 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Strasbourg
locations.story: Where Victor and Clerval meet to begin their journey to England. From here they take a boat down the Rhine, Victor carrying his chemical instruments and the dread of the work still to do.
locations.quote: arrived at Strasburgh, where I waited two days for Clerval

image.caption: The old Maison Kammerzell, Strasbourg — photochrom, c.1895
image.credit: Photochrom Print Collection, c.1895 · Library of Congress / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Rotterdam
locations.story: The end of the Rhine voyage, on the plains of Holland, where Victor and Clerval take ship across the Channel for England.
locations.quote: arrived in a few days at Rotterdam, whence we proceeded by sea to England

image.caption: The Leuvehaven, Rotterdam, thick with sailing barges — photochrom, c.1900
image.credit: Photochrom Print Collection, c.1900 · Library of Congress / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: London
locations.story: The travellers' point of rest for several months. Clerval delights in the city; Victor, dreading his promise, quietly gathers the materials for the second creature before they turn north.
locations.quote: we saw the numerous steeples of London, St. Paul’s towering above all

image.caption: London from the Thames, St Paul's towering over the shipping — oil painting, c.1850
image.credit: John Gendall, c.1850 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Oxford
locations.story: A stop on the leisurely tour north — Windsor, Oxford, Matlock, the lakes. Clerval is happy among the ancient colleges; Victor walks the same streets 'a blasted tree', the bolt already in his soul.
locations.quote: From thence we proceeded to Oxford.

image.caption: Oxford, the High Street and the spires and domes beyond — photochrom, c.1895
image.credit: Photoglob Zürich / Detroit Publishing Co. photochrom, c.1895 · Library of Congress / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Perth
locations.story: Where a Scottish acquaintance expects the two friends. Victor leaves Clerval here to make 'the tour of Scotland alone' — really to finish his loathsome work in solitude — with Perth set as their rendezvous.
locations.quote: along the banks of the Tay, to Perth, where our friend expected us

image.caption: Perth and the River Tay from Bridgend — photochrom, c.1900
image.credit: Photochrom Print Collection, c.1900 · Library of Congress / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: A remote Orkney island
locations.novelName: one of the remotest of the Orkneys
locations.note: Shelley names only 'one of the remotest of the Orkneys' — a bare rock of five inhabitants, three huts, five miles from the mainland. No specific island is given; this point sits among the outer northern isles of Orkney as an editorial best guess. The position is not the novel's.
locations.story: The bleak rock where Victor sets up his laboratory to build the female creature. Half-finished, appalled at what a race of them might do, he tears the thing apart — and looks up to see the creature watching at the window. That night he rows out and sinks her remains at sea, and is carried off course.
locations.quote: one of the remotest of the Orkneys as the scene of my labours

image.caption: The Old Man of Hoy, a bare sea-stack among the remotest of the Orkneys, in a Daniell aquatint of 1817 — the kind of desolate rock where Victor sets up his second laboratory.
image.credit: William Daniell, 'The Old Man of Hoy', 1817 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: A town on the Irish coast
locations.novelName: the town where Mr. Kirwin is magistrate
locations.note: The town is unnamed; the text says only that Victor, adrift from Orkney, is blown to an Irish coast and hauled before the magistrate Mr. Kirwin. Scholarship places the landfall on Ireland's north-west (the plausible drift from Orkney). This point sits on that coast as a best guess; the position is not the novel's.
locations.story: Cast adrift from the Orkneys, Victor drifts to an Irish harbour — where the townsfolk, hostile from the start, accuse him of a murder committed the night before. The body is Clerval's, the fingermarks on its throat the creature's signature. He is imprisoned, falls into fever, and is at last acquitted.
locations.quote: it is the custom of the Irish to hate villains

image.caption: The wild west coast of Ireland under a breaking sky — oil painting, 1913
image.credit: Robert Henri, 'West Coast of Ireland', 1913 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: St Petersburg
locations.story: Where Walton's letters begin. Full of an explorer's hope of a passage to the pole, he writes to his sister before travelling north to hire a ship and crew.
locations.quote: I am already far north of London, and as I walk in the streets of Petersburgh

image.caption: St Petersburg, the Admiralty and the Neva — photochrom, c.1895
image.credit: Photochrom Print Collection, c.1895 · Library of Congress / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Arkhangelsk (Archangel)
locations.story: The White Sea port where Walton fits out his vessel and gathers his sailors. It is also the northern edge of the world Victor crosses on the creature's trail, before both pass out onto the frozen sea.
locations.quote: I have hired a vessel and am occupied in collecting my sailors

image.caption: The White Sea port of Archangel, tall ships at anchor — photograph, c.1900
image.credit: Unknown photographer, c.1900 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: The Black Sea
locations.novelName: a vessel bound for the Black Sea
locations.story: Following the creature down the Rhône to the Mediterranean, Victor watches it board a ship for the Black Sea by night and takes passage in the same vessel — the hinge where the pursuit leaves western Europe and turns toward Russia and the ice.
locations.quote: I saw the fiend enter by night and hide himself in a vessel bound for the Black Sea

image.caption: Ships on the Black Sea at dusk, painted by Ivan Aivazovsky in 1871 — the sea Victor's creature sails for, taking passage in the same vessel.
image.credit: Ivan Aivazovsky, 1871 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: The northern Russian coast
locations.novelName: a wretched hamlet on the seashore
locations.note: The novel gives no name — only 'a wretched hamlet on the seashore' at the far edge of Tartary and Russia, where the creature seizes a dog-sledge and drives out onto the frozen ocean, Victor a day behind. This point stands on the Russian Arctic coast as an illustrative anchor; the position is not the novel's.
locations.story: The last land. Victor reaches a poor coastal hamlet to learn the creature has stolen a sledge and a drove of dogs and struck out across the sea ice the night before. He exchanges his land-sledge for one built for the Frozen Ocean, stocks it, and follows onto the ice.
locations.quote: I exchanged my land-sledge for one fashioned for the inequalities of the Frozen Ocean

image.caption: A hut on a bleak Arctic shore, ice and a ship offshore — 'Coast of Nova Zembla', 1841
image.credit: François-Auguste Biard, 'Côte de la Nouvelle-Zemble', 1841 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: The frozen sea, toward the Pole
locations.novelName: the everlasting ices of the north
locations.note: The novel's ending drifts on pack ice north of Archangel toward the pole, with no fixed position — the ship is beset, Victor dies, the creature vanishes north. This point is an illustrative anchor out on the Arctic ice; the position is not the novel's.
locations.story: Where the two threads finally meet. Walton's ship is frozen fast; out of the white a dog-sledge brings a dying Victor aboard. He tells the whole story and dies; the creature comes to the cabin to mourn its maker, then springs from the window onto an ice raft and is 'lost in darkness and distance'.
locations.quote: I seek the everlasting ices of the north, where you will feel the misery of cold and frost, to which I am impassive

image.caption: A ship beset and crushed in the Arctic pack ice by moonlight — oil painting, c.1846
image.credit: W. H. Smyth, 'Perilous Position of HMS Terror, 1837', c.1846 · Royal Museums Greenwich / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Thurso, on the Caithness coast
locations.note: The novel sends Victor across 'the northern highlands' to 'the coast' to take a boat for the Orkneys but names no town; Thurso is the natural embarkation on the Caithness shore.
locations.story: The north coast of Scotland, where Victor takes a boat out to the remotest of the Orkneys.

image.caption: Thurso across its bay from Holborn Head, in a William Daniell aquatint of c.1820 — the Caithness coast where Victor takes a boat out to the remote Orkneys.
image.credit: William Daniell, from A Voyage Round Great Britain, c.1820 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Havre-de-Grace (Le Havre)
locations.story: The French port where Victor and his father land from Ireland, on the road home to Geneva.
locations.quote: We took our passage on board a vessel bound for Havre-de-Grace

image.caption: The port of Le Havre, painted by Claude Monet in 1874 — where Victor and his father land from Ireland on the road home to Geneva.
image.credit: Claude Monet, 'Port of Le Havre', 1874 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Marseille
locations.note: Victor follows 'the windings of the Rhone' to 'the blue Mediterranean' and takes ship after the creature; the text names no port, but the Rhone reaches the sea at Marseille.
locations.story: Where the Rhone meets the Mediterranean, and Victor takes passage after the creature, bound for the Black Sea.
locations.imageBlank: The text names only 'the blue Mediterranean'; the one period candidate for the conjectured port of Marseille is a heavily-framed salon plate that would need cropping, so the place keeps its pin without a picture.

via.name: The road north
via.note: The winter post-road across Russia, the sledges flying 'quickly over the snow' toward the White Sea.

movements.routeNote: Walton travels overland from St Petersburg to Archangel by the winter post-road — the same road Victor calls 'the post-road between St. Petersburgh and Archangel' — the horses drawing sledges over the snow.
movements.routeSource: No town-by-town route is named; Shelley has Walton write from St Petersburg (Letter 1) then Archangel (Letter 2) and mentions 'the post-road between St. Petersburgh and Archangel'. Reconstructs the standard overland winter route.
movements.note: By sledge over the snow, to fit out his ship

via.name: The Gorlo of the White Sea
via.note: Through the throat of the White Sea into the Barents.

via.name: Out of the White Sea
via.note: Standing north into the Arctic Ocean, bound for the pole.

via.name: Into the pack ice
via.note: The higher latitudes, where the ship is at last beset and frozen fast.

movements.routeNote: Walton sails north from Archangel out of the White Sea and up into the Arctic pack ice, where his ship is frozen in and the sledges appear on the horizon. The exact track is undrawn in the text — a gesture northward.
movements.routeSource: No course is charted in the text; Walton sets out from Archangel toward the pole (Letters 2–4) and is beset by ice. Illustrative northward voyage.
movements.note: North toward the pole, until the ice closes round the ship
Childhood and boyhood at Geneva and Belrive — the still centre before everything

via.name: Strasbourg
via.note: Up out of Switzerland by the Rhine valley, crossing into Bavaria on the road to the university.

via.name: Stuttgart approaches
via.note: Across Württemberg toward the Bavarian plain and Ingolstadt.

movements.routeNote: Victor's 'long and fatiguing' journey from Geneva to Ingolstadt, up through Switzerland and across the German states to Bavaria. The path is not itemised; this reconstructs the natural overland route by Strasbourg and the Rhine.
movements.routeSource: Shelley says only the journey was 'long and fatiguing' (Ch. 3). Reconstructs the standard Geneva–Bavaria overland route.
movements.note: To the university, at seventeen — the road to the making

via.name: The woods and fields
via.note: The creature wanders out of the city into the countryside, wretched and hunted, learning cold and hunger before it finds the cottage.

movements.routeNote: Made at Ingolstadt, the creature flees into the surrounding country and wanders for some days before sheltering by the De Laceys' cottage. The novel gives no map; this is a gesture across the German countryside to a conjectured cottage.
movements.routeSource: The creature's own account (Chs. 11–12) of wandering from the city into the woods and fields to the cottage. No route named; illustrative.
movements.note: Cast out into the wilds; learning to be alive

via.name: Strasbourg
via.note: Back down through the German states toward Switzerland after the news of William's death.

via.name: The gates of Geneva
via.note: Arriving after dark to find the city gates shut; he must wait out a storm on the lakeshore.

movements.routeNote: Word of William's murder brings Victor home by the same overland route in reverse — the German states and Switzerland to Geneva, where he arrives to a locked city and a thunderstorm over the lake.
movements.routeSource: Shelley has Victor summoned home by Elizabeth's letter (Ch. 7); the return traces the Ingolstadt–Geneva road. Reconstructs the overland route.
movements.note: Home to a murdered brother — and, in the lightning, the creature

via.name: Toward Switzerland
via.note: After the De Laceys flee and it burns their cottage, the creature travels toward Geneva, seeking its maker.

movements.routeNote: Rejected and enraged, the creature quits the burnt cottage and travels across country to Geneva, where it comes upon and strangles the child William at Plainpalais. The overland path is unstated — a gesture south to Switzerland.
movements.routeSource: The creature's account (Ch. 16) of travelling toward Geneva and killing William at Plainpalais. No route named; illustrative.
movements.note: To Geneva, and the murder of William — the first blood

via.name: The Arve valley
via.note: South up the valley of the Arve out of Geneva into the Alps toward Chamonix.

movements.routeNote: Victor rides south from Geneva up the Arve valley into the Alps to Chamounix, seeking in the mountains some relief from his grief. The valley road is the only way in; the path is natural rather than itemised.
movements.routeSource: Shelley has Victor journey 'towards the valley of Chamounix' (Ch. 9). The Arve valley is the sole approach. Reconstructs it.
movements.note: Into the Alps, to be alone with the mountains
The climb to the Mer de Glace — where the creature is waiting

via.name: The high Alps
via.note: The creature keeps to the heights and the glaciers, where no one will hunt it, shadowing its maker.

movements.routeNote: The creature haunts the Alpine heights after William's murder and comes down onto the Mer de Glace to confront Victor. It moves through country impassable to ordinary men; the path is a gesture across the high Alps.
movements.routeSource: The creature crosses the ice to meet Victor at Montanvert (Ch. 10). No route named; illustrative.
movements.note: Bounding over the crevasses to meet its maker
Down from the glacier, having heard the creature's tale and agreed to its demand

via.name: The Arve valley
via.note: Back down the Arve to Geneva, weighed with the promise to make a second creature.

movements.routeNote: Victor returns from the Alps to Geneva by the Arve valley, carrying the bargain he has struck. The return traces the outward road.
movements.routeSource: Reconstructs the Chamounix–Geneva valley road, the sole route.
movements.note: Home, bound by his promise

via.name: Basel
via.note: Down out of Switzerland to the Rhine, on the road to Strasbourg where Clerval will join him.

movements.routeNote: Victor sets out for England, travelling from Geneva to Strasbourg where he has arranged to meet Clerval. The overland leg down to the Rhine is reconstructed.
movements.routeSource: Shelley: 'it was arranged that Clerval should join me at Strasburgh' (Ch. 18). Reconstructs the Geneva–Strasbourg road.
movements.note: Setting out for England — to build the mate

via.name: Across Bavaria
via.note: Clerval travels from Ingolstadt to join Victor at Strasbourg for the journey to England.

movements.routeNote: Clerval, who has been at Ingolstadt with Victor, travels to Strasbourg to meet him there, as arranged, before they descend the Rhine together. The overland leg is reconstructed.
movements.routeSource: Shelley, Ch. 18: 'it was arranged that Clerval should join me at Strasburgh.' Reconstructs Clerval's leg to the rendezvous.
movements.note: To join Victor at Strasbourg for the road to England

via.name: Mainz
via.note: Down the Rhine by boat: 'The course of the Rhine below Mainz becomes much more picturesque.'

via.name: Cologne
via.note: 'Beyond Cologne we descended to the plains of Holland' — the last of the gorge country.

movements.routeNote: Victor and Clerval descend the Rhine by boat from Strasbourg through Mainz and Cologne to the plains of Holland and Rotterdam — the very towns Shelley names as they pass.
movements.routeSource: Shelley, Ch. 18: 'descend the Rhine in a boat from Strasburgh to Rotterdam', naming Mainz, Cologne and the plains of Holland.
movements.note: Down the Rhine, past the ruined castles

via.name: The Straits of Dover
via.note: Across the Channel: 'the white cliffs of Britain', then up the Thames past Gravesend, Woolwich and Greenwich.

via.name: Tilbury Fort
via.note: 'We saw Tilbury Fort and remembered the Spanish Armada' — up the Thames toward London.

movements.routeNote: By sea from Rotterdam across the Channel and up the Thames to London — past the white cliffs, Tilbury Fort, Gravesend, Woolwich and Greenwich, every landmark Shelley names on the river approach.
movements.routeSource: Shelley, Ch. 18: 'proceeded by sea to England… the banks of the Thames… Tilbury Fort… Gravesend, Woolwich, and Greenwich… the numerous steeples of London'.
movements.note: Up the Thames to London — a rest of several months

via.name: Windsor
via.note: 'We quitted London on the 27th of March and remained a few days at Windsor, rambling in its beautiful forest.'

movements.routeNote: The tour north begins Windsor then Oxford, exactly as Shelley itemises the route — 'Windsor, Oxford, Matlock, and the Cumberland lakes'.
movements.routeSource: Shelley, Ch. 19: 'to visit Windsor, Oxford, Matlock, and the Cumberland lakes'; 'We quitted London on the 27th of March and remained a few days at Windsor… From thence we proceeded to Oxford.'
movements.note: The leisurely tour north begins

via.name: Matlock
via.note: 'We left Oxford with regret and proceeded to Matlock' — its caves recalling the collections at Chamounix.

via.name: Cumberland & Westmorland
via.note: 'we passed two months in Cumberland and Westmorland' among the lakes, Victor almost cheated into happiness.

via.name: Edinburgh
via.note: 'I visited Edinburgh with languid eyes and mind' — Arthur's Seat, the new town, the castle.

via.name: St Andrews
via.note: 'passing through Coupar, St. Andrew’s, and along the banks of the Tay, to Perth.'

movements.routeNote: Oxford, Matlock, the Cumberland lakes, Edinburgh, Coupar, St Andrews and the Tay to Perth — the whole northward itinerary comes straight from Shelley's own list of stops.
movements.routeSource: Shelley, Ch. 19: Matlock, Cumberland and Westmorland, Edinburgh, then 'through Coupar, St. Andrew’s, and along the banks of the Tay, to Perth, where our friend expected us.'
movements.note: Up through the lakes and Scotland to Perth

movements.routeSource: The novel names only 'the northern highlands' and 'the coast'; reconstructed up through Sutherland to Caithness.
movements.note: Victor crosses the northern Highlands alone to the Caithness coast.

movements.routeSource: The Pentland Firth crossing to the Orkneys.
movements.note: A boat out across the Pentland Firth to one of the remotest of the Orkneys.

movements.routeNote: Adrift from Orkney after sinking the female creature's remains, Victor is blown south-west across open sea and lands on an Irish coast. Shelley gives only 'the land had a wild and rocky appearance' and the town of magistrate Kirwin; the drift and Irish landfall are reconstructed toward the conjectured Irish town.
movements.routeSource: Shelley, Ch. 20–21: Victor cast adrift, then landing at an unnamed Irish town before Mr. Kirwin. No course given; reconstructs the drift from Orkney to Ireland.
movements.note: Cast adrift — and landed to a charge of murder: Clerval's

movements.routeNote: Clerval leaves Perth to rejoin Victor and is murdered by the creature; his body turns up on the Irish coast where Victor lands. The path is entirely off-page — drawn only as a gesture to the place his corpse is found.
movements.routeSource: Shelley, Ch. 21: the murdered man is Clerval, found on the Irish shore. His route is unnarrated; illustrative.
movements.note: To rejoin Victor — strangled by the creature on the way

movements.routeSource: 'We took our passage on board a vessel bound for Havre-de-Grace' — round the south of Ireland and up the Channel.
movements.note: Acquitted, Victor sails from Ireland with his father, bound for Havre-de-Grace.

movements.routeSource: 'we landed at Havre-de-Grace… towards Paris' — then overland to Geneva.
movements.note: Home overland through France, by way of Paris, to Geneva.

via.name: Across the lake
via.note: 'commence our journey by water… we passed the river Drance', coasting under Mont Salêve and the Jura toward Evian.

movements.routeNote: Victor and Elizabeth cross Lake Geneva by boat on their wedding evening to Evian, past Mont Salêve, the Drance and the amphitheatre of mountains — the shoreline Shelley describes as they sail.
movements.routeSource: Shelley, Ch. 22–23: 'commence our journey by water, sleeping that night at Evian'; the lake passage past the Drance and the eastern mountains.
movements.note: The wedding voyage across the lake — where Elizabeth dies
Racing back with Elizabeth's body to warn his father — who dies of the grief

via.name: Shadowing Victor
via.note: The creature follows Victor to the Orkneys and back — it is the window-face on the island, Clerval's killer in Ireland — and comes at last to Evian for the wedding night.

movements.routeNote: From the Alpine meeting the creature shadows Victor across the whole second journey — Orkney, Ireland, home — keeping its threat ('I shall be with you on your wedding-night'), and reaches Evian to strangle Elizabeth. The path is a single illustrative thread standing for a pursuit told only glancingly.
movements.routeSource: The creature's threat and its killings of Clerval (Ireland) and Elizabeth (Evian) are the visible marks of a pursuit Shelley otherwise leaves off-page. Illustrative.
movements.note: Always a step behind Victor — keeping its promise for the wedding night (The Orkney waypoint is deliberate — the creature shadows Victor everywhere; its Channel crossing is a side effect, spill-exempt.)

movements.routeSource: 'I followed the windings of the Rhone' down to the Mediterranean.
movements.note: The pursuit begins: down the windings of the Rhone to the blue Mediterranean.

movements.routeSource: By sea after the creature — through the Mediterranean and the straits into the Black Sea.
movements.note: He takes passage after the creature by night, through the Mediterranean and the straits to the Black Sea.

via.name: The wilds of Tartary and Russia
via.note: 'Amidst the wilds of Tartary and Russia… I have ever followed in his track', reading the print of the creature's step on the snow.

via.name: The frozen north
via.note: Ever northward as the snows thicken, guided by the marks the creature leaves cut in stone and bark.

movements.routeNote: From the Black Sea Victor pursues the creature overland across Tartary and Russia to the Arctic seashore — much of it by dog-sledge over the snow ('quickly over the snow in their sledges'). Shelley names the regions but no towns; reconstructs the northward line.
movements.routeSource: Shelley, Ch. 24: 'Amidst the wilds of Tartary and Russia… I have ever followed in his track… the print of his huge step on the white plain', reaching a hamlet on the northern seashore. Dog-sledge per Ch. 24.
movements.note: By dog-sledge across Tartary and Russia, following the prints in the snow

via.name: Eastward across Europe
via.note: The creature leads Victor on, leaving marks and food to keep the chase alive — 'you live, and my power is complete. Follow me.'

via.name: Into the Russian north
via.note: By dog-sledge across the snow toward the Frozen Ocean, always a day ahead.

movements.routeNote: The creature draws Victor the length of Europe and across Russia to the Arctic coast, taunting him with inscriptions and leaving food so the pursuit will not fail — a dog-sledge over the snow, seizing dogs and provisions at the last hamlet. The path stands in for a route Shelley gives only in gestures.
movements.routeSource: Shelley, Ch. 24: the creature's inscriptions ('Follow me… I seek the everlasting ices of the north') and its theft of a sledge and dogs at the coastal hamlet. Illustrative eastward line.
movements.note: Leading its maker north — a dog-sledge, always one day ahead

via.name: Onto the Frozen Ocean
via.note: Both take dog-sledges out across the mountainous sea ice, the creature a day ahead, Victor gaining — until the ice breaks between them.

movements.routeNote: Creature and pursuer both drive dog-sledges out from the coast onto the Frozen Ocean, north toward the pole, until the breaking ice cuts Victor off and Walton's ship finds him. The track across the pack ice is undrawable — an illustrative line north.
movements.routeSource: Shelley, Ch. 24: 'I exchanged my land-sledge for one fashioned for the inequalities of the Frozen Ocean… I departed from land'; the creature's sledge a day ahead. Illustrative.
movements.note: Dog-sledges across the mountainous ice, toward the pole — where Walton's ship lies beset

story.title: A stranger on the ice
story.narration: The story reaches us third-hand. Robert Walton, an English explorer whose ship lies frozen fast in the Arctic, takes aboard a dying man found adrift on the pack ice — and writes down every word of the tale he tells.

story.title: North to fit out a ship
story.narration: Walton had set out from St Petersburg in high hope, sledging over the winter snow to the White Sea port of Archangel to hire a vessel and a crew for a passage to the pole.

story.title: Into the pack ice
story.narration: He sailed north until the ice closed round the ship and held it fast. There, becalmed in a white waste, the sledges appeared on the horizon, and the ruined figure of Victor Frankenstein was carried up over the side to tell his story.

story.title: The maker's tale
story.narration: This is his story now. Victor Frankenstein was born to a good family in Geneva, and raised beside Elizabeth Lavenza, an orphan cousin taken in as a child — the sister he would come to love, and mean one day to marry.

Wind the clock back, then, to the beginning of it all — to a Geneva boyhood years before the ice, when nothing had gone wrong yet and the whole ruinous story was still only a boy's idle curiosity about the workings of the world.

story.title: A Geneva boyhood
story.narration: The childhood is idyllic — the city house, the lakeside place at Belrive, his friend Henry Clerval. Then a storm splits an oak before the boy's eyes, and he turns his mind to the hidden forces of nature that will one day undo him.

story.title: To the university
story.narration: At seventeen, his mother newly dead of scarlet fever, Victor leaves for the university at Ingolstadt in Bavaria — a long, fatiguing road up out of Switzerland and across the German states to the study of natural philosophy.

story.title: The making
story.narration: Two years he labours in secret, and learns the terrible thing: how to bestow life on dead matter. On a dreary night of November the creature opens its dull yellow eye — and, appalled by what he has made, Victor flees the room.

story.title: The fever, and a letter
story.narration: When he dares return, the room is empty; the creature is gone. Victor collapses into months of brain fever, nursed by the newly arrived Clerval — until a letter comes from home with news that turns the tale to horror.

story.title: Home to a murder
story.narration: His young brother William has been strangled. Victor hurries home the long road in reverse, and reaches Geneva in a night storm — in whose lightning, on the open ground of Plainpalais, he glimpses the creature and knows at once who the murderer is.

story.title: Justine hanged
story.narration: But the law fastens on Justine, the family's gentle servant, on the strength of a trinket found on her. She is condemned and hanged for a killing Victor knows she did not do — and cannot say so without being thought mad.

story.title: Into the Alps
story.narration: Sick with grief and guilt, Victor rides south from Geneva up the valley of the Arve into the mountains at Chamounix, hoping the sublime silence of the Alps will quiet a mind that has begun to break.

story.title: On the Mer de Glace
story.narration: He climbs alone to the great glacier above the valley. There, bounding across the crevasses with a speed no man could match, the creature comes down to meet its maker face to face — and demands, at last, to be heard.

story.title: The creature speaks
story.narration: Now the maker must listen. The creature tells its own story — of waking alone at Ingolstadt, abandoned by the man who made it, knowing nothing, wanted by no one, driven out into the German countryside to learn cold and hunger for itself.

story.title: Cast out into the wilds
story.narration: It wandered from the city into the woods and fields for some days, wretched and hunted from every door, until it found a hovel beside a poor cottage where it might shelter unseen and watch the family within.

story.title: The cottage months
story.narration: For months it watches the blind old De Lacey and his children through a chink in the wall, learning to speak, to read, to long for kindness. When it shows itself, they beat it and flee; it burns the cottage and turns to hatred.

story.title: The first blood
story.narration: It travels across country toward Geneva, seeking the maker it has learned to name. On the ground at Plainpalais it comes upon a beautiful child — Victor's brother William — and, learning whose he is, strangles him, and frames the sleeping Justine for the deed.

Wind back a little, to the days after that murder — before the creature ever came down to the glacier. From William's body it climbed into the high Alps, where no man could follow, and waited there for the maker it meant to confront.

story.title: Up to the ice
story.narration: Keeping to the glaciers and the heights, the creature shadowed Victor's own grieving journey into the mountains, and came down onto the Mer de Glace to meet him — which is where its telling began, and where its demand now falls.

story.title: The demand
story.narration: It asks one thing: a companion, a female of its own kind, as wretched as itself, that the two may quit mankind forever. Grant it, and Victor will never see the creature again. Refuse, and it will turn its rage on all he loves.

story.title: The bargain
story.narration: Victor takes the tale back into his own hands. Half in pity, half in dread, he consents to make the thing a mate — and the two part on the ice, the maker bound now to a promise he already loathes.

story.title: Down from the glacier
story.narration: He descends from the Mer de Glace to Chamounix, the bargain sealed and the weight of it already settling on him — a second creature to be built, and a race of them, perhaps, to follow.

story.title: Home, and delaying
story.narration: Back down the Arve valley to Geneva he goes, carrying his promise. For months he puts off the loathsome work, until his father, mistaking his gloom for reluctance to marry, presses on with the wedding to Elizabeth.

story.title: Setting out for England
story.narration: To finish the work far from home, Victor sets out for England, travelling down out of Switzerland to Strasbourg on the Rhine, where he has arranged for Clerval to join him for the journey.
And on the same days, from the university, Clerval sets out for the rendezvous — the one friend still at Victor's side, glad of the tour, knowing nothing of the true and dreadful purpose behind it.

story.title: Clerval joins him
story.narration: Henry Clerval travels from Ingolstadt across Bavaria to Strasbourg, where the two friends meet as arranged and turn together toward the Rhine and the long way down to England.

story.title: Down the Rhine
story.narration: The two take a boat down the Rhine — past Mainz and Cologne and the ruined castles above the gorge, down at last to the flat plains of Holland and Rotterdam. Clerval is happy; Victor carries his instruments and his dread.

story.title: Up the Thames
story.narration: By sea across the Channel and up the Thames to London — past the white cliffs, Tilbury Fort, the steeples of the city. They rest here some months; Clerval delights in it while Victor quietly gathers materials for the second creature.

story.title: The tour north begins
story.narration: They quit London in the spring for a leisurely tour — Windsor first, then Oxford, where Clerval is happy among the ancient colleges and Victor walks the same streets like a blasted tree, the bolt already in his soul.

story.title: Through the lakes to Scotland
story.narration: On through Matlock, the Cumberland lakes, Edinburgh and St Andrews to Perth, where a friend expects them. Among the mountains Victor is almost cheated into happiness — but the work still waits, and it must be done alone.

story.title: Alone to the coast
story.narration: Leaving Clerval at Perth, Victor crosses the northern Highlands alone to the Caithness coast, sick of the work he has promised and the second creature he must make.

story.title: A bare rock
story.narration: From there he takes a boat out to one of the remotest of the Orkneys — a bare rock of three wretched huts — and sets up his laboratory to make the she-creature the monster has demanded.

story.title: The female destroyed
story.narration: Half-finished, he thinks what a race of such beings might loose upon the world — and looks up to see the creature grinning at the window. In a passion he tears the female apart. It vanishes, vowing: 'I shall be with you on your wedding-night.'

story.title: Cast adrift
story.narration: That night he rows out and sinks the remains at sea, then sleeps in the boat and wakes far from land, a rising wind carrying him he knows not where. He makes a small Irish harbour — and is seized by a hostile crowd.

story.title: Clerval's last journey
story.narration: For a body was found on the shore the night before, the marks of a great hand livid on its throat. Turn to the dead man: it is Henry Clerval, who had left Perth to rejoin his friend, and met the creature on the way.
His last road runs almost wholly off the page. Wind back to Perth, where he parted from Victor, and follow the friend who trusted the world to the place where the creature was waiting for him.

story.title: To rejoin his friend
story.narration: From Perth, Clerval set out to catch up with Victor — until the creature's hands closed on his throat, and his body was carried to the very Irish coast where his friend, days later, would come ashore accused of the murder.

story.title: Acquitted, and away
story.narration: Victor is thrown into prison and a raving fever, and acquitted only when the dates clear him of Clerval's murder. Broken, he sails from Ireland with his father, bound for Havre-de-Grace.

story.title: Home to a wedding
story.narration: They land in France and travel home overland by way of Paris to Geneva — where a wedding to Elizabeth is waiting, and the creature's promise to be with him on his wedding night.

story.title: The wedding voyage
story.narration: Married at last, the two cross Lake Geneva by boat on their wedding evening, coasting under Mont Salêve toward Evian. Victor takes the creature's threat as meant for himself, and keeps a pistol close, watchful of every shadow on the water.

story.title: The wedding night
story.narration: At the inn he sends Elizabeth ahead and paces the halls, listening. Too late he understands whom the vow was for: a scream from the room he has left her in, and she lies dead across the bed, the creature grinning through the open window.

For while Victor toiled at his mate and wandered from Orkney to Ireland and home, the creature shadowed him throughout. Wind back to the glacier where they parted, and follow the thread that kept the promise made upon the ice.

story.title: Keeping its promise
story.narration: From the Alpine meeting the creature had followed Victor across the whole second journey — the face at the Orkney window, the killer on the Irish shore — always a step behind, until it reached Evian and made good the threat sworn upon the ice.

Return to Victor at Evian, with nothing left to lose. He races back across the lake with Elizabeth's body; his father dies of the grief within days. Alone in the world, he swears his life to one end: the hunting-down of the thing he made.

story.title: The last of the family
story.narration: Back across the lake to Geneva he carries his terrible news. Having buried Elizabeth, his father, and every soul he loved, Victor turns from the graves with no thought left in him but the pursuit of the creature to the ends of the earth.

story.title: Down the Rhône
story.narration: Elizabeth murdered and his father dead of grief, Victor has nothing left but the hunt. He follows the windings of the Rhône down to the blue Mediterranean, tracking the fiend by the clues it leaves to keep the chase alive.

story.title: Out to sea, after him
story.narration: At the coast he sees the creature slip aboard a ship by night, bound for the Black Sea, and takes passage after it — through the Mediterranean and the straits, always a day behind.

story.title: Across Tartary and Russia
story.narration: Across the wilds of Tartary and Russia the chase runs north, much of it by dog-sledge over the snow — Victor reading the print of the creature's huge step on the white plain, guided on by taunts it leaves cut in bark and stone.

The two are so bound now they might be one journey. Wind back to the creature's own line east across Europe and Russia — the quarry that drew its hunter on, leaving marks and even food so the pursuit should never fail.

story.title: Leading its maker north
story.narration: From Evian the creature drew Victor the length of Europe and across Russia to the Arctic shore — always a day ahead, taunting him onward: 'You live, and my power is complete. Follow me; I seek the everlasting ices of the north.'

And at the last hamlet on the seashore the two threads finally run together, out onto the frozen ocean itself — the creature a day ahead on a stolen sledge, Victor close behind, Walton's beset ship somewhere in the white beyond.

story.title: Onto the frozen sea
story.narration: The creature seizes a sledge and dogs and strikes out onto the Frozen Ocean; Victor trades his land-sledge for one built for the ice and follows. Both drive north across the pack — until the ice breaks between them, and Walton's ship lies dead ahead.

story.title: The frame closes
story.narration: And so the tale catches up with the frozen ship where it began. Walton takes the story up again: Victor, worn out and past saving, finishes his telling and dies in the cabin, still charging the captain to destroy the creature if ever he can.

story.title: Lost in darkness and distance
story.narration: That night the creature comes to mourn its maker, grieving with a wretchedness no one thought it could feel. Then it springs from the window onto an ice-raft and is borne away, to seek its own end in fire — 'lost in darkness and distance'.
